title: Asperula capillacea
intro: Asperula capillacea, commonly known as Woodruff, is a low-growing, perennial herb native to Europe and Western Asia, characterized by its small white or pink flowers and distinctive, sweetly scented foliage. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues, reduce fever, and promote wound healing. Bioactive constituents of Asperula capillacea include iridoid glycosides, phenolic acids, and flavonoids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. Asperula capillacea is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being a popular ingredient in traditional remedies and perfumes, such as May wine.
taxonomy: Asperula capillacea belongs to the family Rubiaceae, a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as the coffee or madder family. This species is also classified as part of the genus Asperula, which consists of various species of herbs and shrubs found in different parts of the world. The Asperula genus is further classified under the order Gentianales, which includes several other families of flowering plants. At the kingdom level, Asperula capillacea is classified as Plantae, indicating its status as a plant. Overall, the classification of Asperula capillacea reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships with other plant species.
uses: Asperula capillacea, also known as creeping woodruff, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. Its leaves and stems are said to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in soothing digestive issues and skin problems. The plant is also believed to have antimicrobial properties, making it potentially useful in treating infections and wounds. Additionally, creeping woodruff is thought to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which may help alleviate anxiety and stress. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Asperula capillacea.

The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems, respiratory issues, and skin conditions. Its antioxidant properties are also believed to contribute to its therapeutic benefits, helping to protect against cell damage and inflammation. preparations: Asperula capillacea, commonly known as Wood Sorrel, has been used in herbal preparations for its medicinal properties. The plant's leaves and stems are used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures. In traditional medicine, Asperula capillacea is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. Herbal preparations made from the plant are also used to treat digestive issues and skin conditions. side_effects: Asperula capillacea is a plant species for which there is limited information available.
